channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Two New Cuphead Games Are On The Way, And One’s An 8-Bit Platform Shooter
item.content:encoded:
Studio MDHR brought puppets to Summer Game Fest this time, alongside the news that it’s making not one, but two brand new Cuphead games…and one’s not what you’d expect.

The first to be announced, with no title or release window to be had, is just “a new, hand-animated Cuphead.” That’s it. We didn’t see anything of it or learn anything more, it’s just going to be another game like Cuphead. Neat! Just, non-specific. It sounds like development may have only started recently, so we might have to wait a while to hear more.

The second game is entitled Mighty Cuphead Adventure, and it’s an 8-bit platform shooter that looks to be treading some of the same ground as the original Cuphead, with some familiar-looking bosses and both Cuphead and Mugman starring. According to a press release, Mighty Cuphead Adventure was actually developed with ’80s technology as a tribute to the games of the era, programmed in classic Assembly Language and designed within the specifications of the Sega Master System. Though it’s coming to modern consoles and PC, there will also be a physical Sega Master System cartridge available for anyone who’s still got one of those consoles lying around. No release window for this one either, though PR says to expect more news in the months to come.

We’ve been wondering what Studio MDHR has been up to since it wrapped up Cuphead, which first came out in 2017 and has since gotten multiple ports and a big DLC expansion entitled The Delicious Last Course, which dropped in 2022. All were very well-received and praised for the hand-drawn animation and commitment to a classic ’30s art style and sound. The game got a TV show, The Cuphead Show!, also in 2022, which was fine.



https://kotaku.com/two-new-cuphead-games-hand-drawn-mighty-cuphead-adventure-2000703098
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Next Big Resident Evil Remake Is Code Veronica
item.content:encoded:
The next big Resident Evil remake isn’t Resident Evil 5. Nope! Today at Summer Game Fest, Capcom announced that it is remaking Resident Evil: Code Veronica. And it is out in 2027.

In the trailer released during the event, the game seems to be first-person. We see Claire Redfield entering a Paris hotel where her brother, Chris, is staying before being grabbed by someone. The game is simply called Resident Evil: Veronica. 

For many Resident Evil fans, Code Veronica is the true Resident Evil 3. It’s set a few months after Resident Evil 2 and was released in 2000 for the Sega Dreamcast. It follows Claire and Chris Redfield as they deal with a virus spreading outside Raccoon City. It was the first Resident Evil game that debuted on a non-PlayStation console, and it also did a lot to expand the scope of the franchise. It’s a very important game, but one which has sometimes felt like a forgotten stepchild. That changes now that, following lots of rumors and fan pleading, Capcom has announced that it is remaking Resident Evil: Code Veronica from the ground up.

It’s the latest Resident Evil remake from Capcom, a publisher that has been on a roll recently. Will this be another big hit for the company? In any case, a lot of Resident Evil sickos are super happy to see Code Veronica finally getting the love it deserves.

Resident Evil: Veronica is launching on consoles and PC in 2027.



https://kotaku.com/the-next-big-resident-evil-remake-is-code-veronica-2000703078
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Geoff Keighley Reveals The Final Summer Game Fest Trailer Won’t Be An Online Shooter
item.content:encoded:
Last year’s Game Awards infamously ended with a trailer for Highguard, a live-service free-to-play shooter that didn’t even stick around for three full months before being shut down due to low player count. For Summer Game Fest 2026, show creator and host Geoff Keighley is trying to avoid a repeat of that situation.

On June 5, about an hour before the event was set to kick off, Keighley teased on Twitter that the final reveal of the event wouldn’t be an online live-service multiplayer game like Highguard.

“Today’s final announcement at #SummerGamefest will be a single-player, narrative game,” posted Keighley. “Thank you for the feedback!”

It is for sure a direct reference to the very negative reaction Highguard received from people online in the hours after its initial reveal at the Game Awards 2025. A lot of fans expect the final trailer of these big Geoff events to be something like a new God of War or Last of Us. You know, something big, part of an established franchise, or from a developer with a long track record of hits. And mostly, they don’t want a multiplayer-only character shooter to end the event. It seems Geoff has gotten the memo.

Now, the game begins: What will that final mystery reveal be? What single-player narrative game will Keighley present to fans to wrap up Summer Game Fest 2026?



https://kotaku.com/geoff-keighley-reveals-the-final-summer-game-fest-trailer-wont-be-an-online-shooter-2000703064
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Robot Kicks Small Child In Stomach During Event
item.content:encoded:
Folks, I’m sorry to report that, once again, the robots are up to some bullshit. This time, it involves a humanoid robot wearing a blue clown wig who was recorded kicking a small child right in the gut, causing the young kid to double over in pain as other children nearby laughed.

As reported by Futurism, the incident of robot violence occurred earlier this week during an event in Xinjiang, China. The Shanghai Daily newspaper reported that the young boy was not seriously hurt by the kick, but the mother claimed staff were “slow to react” after the bot attack.

You know who wasn’t slow to react? The kids around the poor boy who started giggling and laughing. Cold, but I have to admit, if I were there and much younger, I too would laugh my ass off at a friend or classmate getting roundhouse-kicked by a robot in a clown wig. Heck, hearing that the kid is okay and doing well, I’ll admit I snickered a bit watching the clip again. That kid just folds up after the kick. Damn. That robot wasn’t holding back.

Of course, if you’ve been reading Kotaku in the past, you’ll be well aware that this isn’t the first time we’ve seen robots go wild and be shitty. In fact, it’s become a bit of a beat around these parts. Somehow, I became the guy who covers robots doing dumb shit or failing in spectacular ways. Not sure how that happened, but hey, I’ll take it.

Anyway, I can’t wait for the next sign of the upcoming robot uprising. Sure, we’ll all perish under the steel feet of our robotic overlords one day, but at least we get to enjoy some silly videos between now and then. That’s something, right?



https://kotaku.com/robot-kicks-small-child-in-stomach-during-event-2000703013
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
